quote: Hey. This is Parker. I'm with Ion. I was just reaching out because we had a request here that you were looking at solar for the home. How you doing?
stewart_read: Parker's intro contains none of the legitimacy anchors the codex rates as the primary scam-anxiety defense: no Tesla-certified framing, no form-fill reference, no state mention. 'A request here' is vague enough to read as a cold call. For a customer demographic that skews 45-70 and carries door-knocker anxiety, the first 30 seconds are the only window to answer 'is this a scam?' before the customer's guard goes up. Parker spent that window on nothing.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have known this was not a Tesla-sourced lead and defaulted to the standard intro — but even the standard intro requires a form-fill reference and state mention, neither of which appeared.
coaching_implication: Pull the lead source on this call with Parker. If it was a Tesla lead, walk through intros.tesla line by line — the legitimacy framing is the entire first-30-seconds job. If it was a standard lead, walk through intros.standard — 'a request here' is not a form-fill reference. Either way, Parker needs to understand WHY the legitimacy anchor exists before the next call.

quote: Is this something you're still looking at, sir?
stewart_read: Bernard answers 'Oh, sure. Yeah.' and Parker moves on. The codex marks interest_in_solar as the highest-weighted verify item — depth_grading: true, 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR. Depth matters more than the answer. Top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' Parker got a one-word confirmation and checked the box. There is no emotional anchor on this call — no bill pain story, no neighbor influence, no environmental motive. The entire pitch that follows has no emotional foundation.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have read Bernard's low energy as a signal to keep moving rather than dig. Strategic read: preserve momentum by not over-probing a passive customer. Rejected — the codex is explicit that depth here is the job, and Bernard's subsequent animation at 02:16 ('Oh my god') shows the emotional material was available if Parker had opened the door.
coaching_implication: Show Parker the contrast between 00:24 (one-word confirm, move on) and 02:16 (Bernard animates on bill pain unprompted). The emotional material was there — Parker just didn't go looking for it. In the 1-on-1, ask: 'What would you have learned if you'd asked Bernard what specifically about the shop made him think about solar?' That's the question that opens the anchor.

quote: Oh my god. I I'm paying I've got two two, gloves. One's, commercial, you know, because of that child. Anyway, it's two phase. So I have both.
stewart_read: This is the only moment of genuine emotional energy on the call. Bernard's 'Oh my god' opener signals real bill frustration — the exact emotional state the codex says should be flipped into alignment-against-the-utility. Parker's response is to estimate a combined total and move on ('Wow. That's a lot. Yeah. We'll definitely see what we can do to save you some money there.'). The bill is captured as data and dropped. No flip, no emotional alignment, no curiosity created. The codex's failure pattern — 'ask + acknowledge + move on' — is exactly what happened here.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have been managing call length given Bernard's low energy. Rejected — Bernard's animation here was the highest-energy moment on the call. This was the moment to slow down, not speed up.
coaching_implication: Walk Parker through coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion with this specific moment. The emotion was handed to him — 'Oh my god' is the customer doing the flip setup. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): something like 'That's what PG&E takes from you every month just to keep the lights on — and that's before the shop. The question our specialist answers is what that looks like when it stops.' Note: this leverage point would land harder if Parker had the actual bill in hand — coaching gap on bill-collection path.

quote: Yeah. We have battery options that can go with solar.
stewart_read: Bernard asks unprompted about storing electricity for the shop — a direct battery storage inquiry. Parker confirms Ion has battery options and asks if Bernard wants to look at that too. Bernard says yes. Parker then moves on without capturing what the storage need is (shop-specific? whole-property? backup vs. offset?), without naming the Tesla Powerwall as the specific product Ion carries, and without flagging this as a cross-sell signal for the closer. The enthusiasm signal is real — a customer who asks about battery storage before the pitch has even started is more qualified, not less. Parker acknowledged it but didn't anchor on it.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have intentionally kept this brief to avoid scope creep into closer-tier product detail. Partially valid — the setter should not spec out a battery system. But acknowledging the Powerwall by name (product accuracy, not brand trust) and flagging the shop-specific storage need for the closer is setter-scope work, not closer-scope work.
coaching_implication: Show Parker that 'we have battery options' is the floor, not the ceiling, for this moment. The codex confirms Tesla Powerwall is the only battery brand Ion carries — naming it is accurate product information, not a sales pitch. More importantly, Parker should have asked one clarifying question: 'Is the storage need mainly for the shop, the house, or both?' That answer shapes the closer's proposal and protects the sit.

quote: There is just two of us. The kids are
stewart_read: Bernard's sentence is cut off — 'the kids are' trails off without completion. Parker does not follow up. The codex requires title_co_owners to be confirmed and, if a co-decision-maker is present, they must be included in the appointment. Parker has no idea who the second person is, whether they're on the title, or whether they'll be present for the closer's call. The incomplete sentence is ambiguous — the second person could be a spouse, an adult child on the title, or simply a reference to grown children who've moved out. That ambiguity is unresolved.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have read 'just two of us' as confirmation of a two-person household with no additional decision-makers. Rejected — the sentence was cut off before Bernard finished the thought, and the codex is explicit that title_co_owners must be confirmed, not inferred.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Parker: 'Who is the second person in that house?' Parker won't know. That's the coaching moment. On the callback, Parker needs to complete this verify item before booking: 'You mentioned there are two of you — is your partner or anyone else on the title with you? We want to make sure the right people are on the appointment.'

quote: the last thing we need from you, Bernard, is what we do to make sure your design is accurate is we take a look at your PG and E bill so we can make sure it's specific to your home. Do you know do you normally get that by email or by paper? How do you normally get that bill?
stewart_read: Bernard's response reveals he receives a paper copy under an auto-pay agreement and has to 'save them all' — a paper-billing, likely elderly customer who is also about to leave the house. Parker's follow-up ('Would you grab that for me real quick?') routes to the photo-send path, which requires Bernard to locate a paper bill, photograph it, and send it — a multi-step process for a customer who is already signaling time pressure. The codex's elderly_low_tech_protocol is explicit: pivot to verbal read-back within 2 minutes of friction, not 10. Parker never pivoted — he asked Bernard to grab the bill, Bernard said he was leaving, and the call ended.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have hoped Bernard could grab the bill quickly before leaving. Reactive read holds — there was no strategic setup here. The bill path chosen was mismatched to the customer's situation, and the mismatch is what ended the call.
coaching_implication: Walk Parker through bill_collection.alternative_paths with this specific customer profile: paper billing + auto-pay + time pressure = verbal read-back or defer to specialist. The suggested pivot (intent, not verbatim): 'No problem — when I call you tomorrow, just have the bill in front of you and read me the kilowatt-hour numbers off the graph. Takes about 30 seconds.' That path keeps the callback alive and sets a concrete task for Bernard before the next call.

quote: Okay. I could call you another day.
stewart_read: Parker releases the call with 'I can call you tomorrow' — no specific time, no 'is that fair' handshake, no computer/no-driving anchor, no text follow-up promise. The codex's button_up section requires all of these elements precisely because their absence predicts no-shows. 'Tomorrow' is not a scheduled appointment. Bernard's re-engagement warmth after his errand is unknown. This is a soft callback, not a sit.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have judged that pushing for a locked time would feel aggressive given Bernard's stated time pressure. Partially valid — but the codex's time_constraint protocol is explicit: anchor the time confidently. 'Vagueness = no-show.' A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'I'll call you tomorrow — morning or afternoon work better for you?' That's one question, takes five seconds, and converts a soft callback into a scheduled one.
coaching_implication: This is the paycheck-protection moment. Per the codex's setter_compensation_model, Parker is paid on sits — a callback that doesn't convert to a sit doesn't pay. Show Parker the delta between 'I'll call you tomorrow' and a locked time with a verbal handshake. The button_up checklist exists for this exact scenario.

quote: especially my, shop broke. It's all steel. You know? So
stewart_read: Bernard volunteers the shop detail in the first 30 seconds — a commercial two-phase account with steel construction. Parker acknowledges ('Oh, your shop is all steel?') and immediately pivots to utility company. The commercial account is a distinct qualification question: Ion's residential solar program may not cover a commercial two-phase service. Parker never asked whether the shop is a separate structure, whether it's on the same property, or whether it's the primary driver of Bernard's solar interest. The closer will walk in without knowing what they're being asked to design for.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have intended to capture the shop details during bill collection. Reactive read holds — the shop was never revisited, and the bill collection attempt ended without capturing either account separately.
coaching_implication: On the callback, Parker needs one clarifying question before anything else: 'The shop you mentioned — is that on the same property as the house, and is it on the same PG&E account or a separate one?' The answer determines whether this is a residential solar lead, a commercial referral, or a dual-account design conversation. The closer cannot walk in without this.
